file_name: Data Use Agreement New York City.txt

perturbation.original_text: Upon becoming aware of such a material breach, DOHMH may do one or more of the following: 1. Provide an opportunity for Data Recipient to cure the violation within 30 days, and terminate the Agreement if Data Recipient does not cure or end the violation within the time speci fied by DOHMH
perturbation.changed_text: Upon becoming aware of such a material breach, DOHMH may do one or more of the following: 1. Provide an opportunity for Data Recipient to cure the violation within 10 days, and terminate the Agreement if Data Recipient does not cure or end the violation within the time speci fied by DOHMH
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period from 30 days to 10 days may be considered unreasonable, especially for complex violations, and could conflict with principles of fairness and good faith implied in contracts in New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding reasonable cure periods in contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While N.Y. U.C.C. § 2-608 specifically addresses revocation of acceptance in sales contracts, it embodies the broader principle that a buyer (or in this case, a Data Recipient) should be afforded a reasonable time to cure a defect or breach. Shortening the cure period to 10 days could be deemed commercially unreasonable, especially if the breach involves complex data handling procedures, thus conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under New York contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 6 Breach, Repudiation and Excuse previous SECTION 2-607 Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach After Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerabl... up PART 6 Breach, Repudiation and Excuse next SECTION 2-609 Right to Adequate Assurance of Performance This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-608 Revocation of Acceptance in Whole or in Part Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 6 Section 2--608. Revocation of Acceptance in Whole or in Part. (1) The buyer may revoke his acceptance of a lot or commercial unit whose non-conformity substantially impairs its value to him if he has accepted it (a) on the reasonable assumption that its non-conformity would be cured and it has not been seasonably cured; or (b) without discovery of such non-conformity if his acceptance was reasonably induced either by the difficulty of discovery before acceptance or by the seller's assurances. (2) Revocation of acceptance must occur within a reasonable time after the buyer discovers or should have discovered the ground for it and before any substantial change in condition of the goods which is not caused by their own defects. It is not effective until the buyer notifies the seller of it. (3) A buyer who so revokes has the same rights and duties with regard to the goods involved as if he had rejected them.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from 30 days to 10 days, which could be considered commercially unreasonable, especially if the breach involves complex data handling procedures. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (N.Y. U.C.C. § 2-608) specifically addresses revocation of acceptance in sales contracts, it embodies the broader principle that a buyer (or in this case, a Data Recipient) should be afforded a reasonable time to cure a defect or breach. Shortening the cure period to 10 days conflicts with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under New York contract law, since a reasonable amount of time must be given. The original contract allowed for 30 days to cure the violation, while the changed contract reduces the amount of time to 10 days.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient will confirm in writing to DOHMH Data Recipient’s destruction or return of Data , and all copies, within 6 0 days of the termination of this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient will confirm in writing to DOHMH Data Recipient’s destruction or return of Data , and all copies, within 90 days of the termination of this Agreement .
perturbation.explanation: Extending the deadline for confirming data destruction to 90 days after termination increases the risk of data breaches and non-compliance with data protection standards, potentially conflicting with HIPAA regulations if health information is involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) Security Rule
perturbation.law_explanation: 45 CFR § 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(B) requires covered entities and business associates to implement policies and procedures to address the final disposition of electronic protected health information (ePHI). While it doesn't specify an exact timeframe, a delay of 90 days in confirming data destruction increases the potential for unauthorized access or disclosure of ePHI, which could be deemed as a failure to implement reasonable and appropriate security measures under HIPAA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: the provisions of the Security Rule. 73 Documentation. A regulated entity must maintain documentation required for written policies and procedures implemented to comply with the Security Rule and actions, activities, or assessments required by the Security Rule to be documented until six years after the later of: 1) the date of the document’s creation or 2) the date the document was last in effect. 74 Availability. A regulated entity must make the written policies and procedures it implements to comply with the Security Rule and its documentation of required actions, activities, and assessments available to those persons responsible for implementing the procedures described in the documentation. 75 Updates. A regulated entity must periodically review and update its documentation in response to environmental or organizational changes that affect the security of ePHI. 76 Endnotes [1]Subtitle F of title II of HIPAA (Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the data destruction confirmation deadline from 60 to 90 days post-agreement termination. The Law Explanation states that a delay increases the potential for unauthorized access or disclosure of ePHI, which could be seen as a failure to implement reasonable security measures under HIPAA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references endnote 39, which corresponds to 45 CFR 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(B). This regulation likely outlines security management process implementation, potentially being undermined by the extended deadline, as the extended window increases the risk of unauthorized access or disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient shall make such report to the designated privacy officer of DOHMH (per the Notice specifications of Section IX of this Agreement) , in writing, within 3 business days after Data Recipient becomes aware of the unauthorized or inadvertent use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient shall make such report to the designated privacy officer of DOHMH (per the Notice specifications of Section IX of this Agreement) , in writing, within 7 business days after Data Recipient becomes aware of the unauthorized or inadvertent use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting timeframe from 3 business days to 7 business days for unauthorized data use or disclosure can conflict with HIPAA's breach notification requirements, which mandate a shorter reporting window to mitigate harm.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) Breach Notification Rule
perturbation.law_explanation: 45 CFR § 164.410 outlines the requirements for breach notification following the discovery of a breach of unsecured protected health information (PHI). While the rule does not specify a strict 3-day reporting deadline to the covered entity or business associate, delaying the initial report could hinder the covered entity's ability to comply with the 60-day notification requirement to affected individuals and the Department of Health and Human Services.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Breach Notification Rule | HHS.gov Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: Breach Notification Rule The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414, requires HIPAA covered entities and their business associates to provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. Similar breach notification provisions implemented and enforced by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) , apply to vendors of personal health records and their third party service providers, pursuant to section 13407 of the HITECH Act. Definition of Breach A breach is, generally, an impermissible use or disclosure under the Privacy Rule that compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information. An impermissible use or disclosure of protected health information is presumed to be a breach unless the covered entity or business associate, as applicable, demonstrates that there is a low probability that the protected health information has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least the following factors: The nature and extent of the protected health information involved, including the types of identifiers and the likelihood of re-identification; The unauthorized person who used the protected health information or to whom the disclosure was made; Whether the protected health information was actually acquired or viewed; and The extent to which the risk to the protected health information has been mitigated. Covered entities and business associates, where applicable, have discretion to provide the required breach notifications following an impermissible use or disclosure without performing a risk assessment to determine the probability that the protected health information has been compromised. There are three exceptions to the definition of “breach.” The first exception applies to the unintentional acquisition, access, or use of protected health information by a workforce member or person acting under the authority of a covered entity or business associate, if such acquisition, access, or use was made in good faith and within the scope of authority. The second exception applies
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the reporting timeframe for unauthorized data use from 3 to 7 business days, which contradicts the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule. While 45 CFR § 164.410 doesn't specify a 3-day deadline, delaying the report could hinder the covered entity's ability to comply with the 60-day notification requirement, as per the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414, requires notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information, suggesting a need for timely reporting to facilitate compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient will notify DOHMH of any changes to Attachment C within 5 days of any changes to the Authorized User list. In addition, Data Recipient will provide DOHMH with any newly entered Authorized User Agreement DOHMH Standard DUA /NDA 5-16-2014 Page 5 with an employee and/or consultant within 5 days of entering such an agreement pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient will notify DOHMH of any changes to Attachment C within 15 days of any changes to the Authorized User list. In addition, Data Recipient will provide DOHMH with any newly entered Authorized User Agreement DOHMH Standard DUA /NDA 5-16-2014 Page 5 with an employee and/or consultant within 15 days of entering such an agreement pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the notification period for changes to authorized users from 5 days to 15 days increases the risk of unauthorized data access, potentially violating New York State's data security laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 899-bb requires businesses to implement reasonable security measures to protect private information. While it doesn't prescribe an exact timeframe for updating authorized user lists, a 15-day delay could be considered unreasonable, especially if it allows former employees or unauthorized individuals to retain access to sensitive data, therefore falling short of the 'reasonable security' standard required by the SHIELD Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: IAPP You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the notification period from 5 to 15 days, potentially increasing the risk of unauthorized data access. The Law Explanation states that N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 899-bb requires businesses to implement 'reasonable security measures' to protect private information. While the law doesn't specify a timeframe, a 15-day delay may be considered unreasonable, as it could allow unauthorized individuals to retain access to sensitive data, thereby potentially violating the 'reasonable security' standard.

perturbation.original_text: Prior to publication or public presentation of such work product, the Data Recipient will submit its final work product to the DOHMH for review and approval. If the work product: 2. Is to be a work of an academic institution or scholar associated with an academic institution, then the conclusions reach ed in academic publications are subject to the DOHMH prior review and comment before publication or public presentation, with turnaround time of each successive draft being no longer than 30 days, unless otherwise specified in Attachment B ; but it is provi de, further, however, that if the Data Recipient and the DOHMH cannot come to an accord about the content thereof, then the academic institution or the academician must allow DOHMH to include a written dissenting opinion in the form required by DOHMH as to the conclusions/findings reached by the work product in publications and in oral presentations.
perturbation.changed_text: Prior to publication or public presentation of such work product, the Data Recipient will submit its final work product to the DOHMH for review and approval. If the work product: 2. Is to be a work of an academic institution or scholar associated with an academic institution, then the conclusions reach ed in academic publications are subject to the DOHMH prior review and comment before publication or public presentation, with turnaround time of each successive draft being no longer than 10 days, unless otherwise specified in Attachment B ; but it is provi de, further, however, that if the Data Recipient and the DOHMH cannot come to an accord about the content thereof, then the academic institution or the academician must allow DOHMH to include a written dissenting opinion in the form required by DOHMH as to the conclusions/findings reached by the work product in publications and in oral presentations.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the turnaround time for DOHMH review and comment on academic publications from 30 days to 10 days could be deemed an unreasonable constraint on academic freedom, potentially conflicting with First Amendment principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: First Amendment of the US Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: The First Amendment protects academic freedom, including the freedom of researchers to disseminate their findings. While DOHMH has a legitimate interest in ensuring data security and accuracy, a 10-day review period may be insufficient for thorough scholarly review, potentially chilling academic expression and violating the spirit of academic freedom protected by the First Amendment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: First Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you First Amendment The First Amendment guarantees freedoms concerning religion, expression, assembly, and the right to petition. It forbids Congress from both promoting one religion over others and also restricting an individual’s religious practices . It guarantees freedom of expression by prohibiting Congress from restricting the press or the rights of individuals to speak freely. It also guarantees the right of citizens to assemble peaceably and to petition their government . Learn more... perturbation.justification: The change from a 30-day review period to a 10-day review period for academic publications creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that a 10-day review period may be insufficient for thorough scholarly review, potentially chilling academic expression and violating the spirit of academic freedom protected by the First Amendment. Scraped Law Snippet 2 affirms that the First Amendment guarantees freedom of expression, including freedom of speech and of the press. By reducing the review period, the modified contract text potentially infringes upon this freedom by hindering the ability of academics to thoroughly vet their work, thus contradicting the principles of the First Amendment.
